canonical_summary:
The FAA AC 450 series is the set of Advisory Circulars published by the FAA Office of Commercial Space Transportation (AST) that provide accepted means of compliance with 14 CFR Part 450, the performance based rule governing US commercial launch and reentry vehicle operator licenses. Each AC maps to one or more specific sections of Part 450, ranging from system safety (450.103) through flight safety analysis (450.115 to 450.139), flight commit criteria (450.165), collision avoidance (450.169), ground safety (450.179), toxic hazards (450.139 and 450.187), and the mishap plan (450.173). All current ACs are listed on the FAA's Commercial Space Advisory Circulars index and are non binding, meaning an applicant may propose alternatives that the FAA finds acceptable.

The complete US authoritative set, as of late 2025, includes AC 413.13-1 (complete application), AC 450.3-1 (scope), AC 450.31-1 (policy and payload), AC 450.103-1 (system safety program), AC 450.108-1 (flight abort), AC 450.109-1 (flight hazard analysis), AC 450.115-1B (high fidelity FSA), AC 450.115-2 (FSA methods description), AC 450.117-1 (trajectory analysis), AC 450.123-1 (population exposure), AC 450.137-1 (de minimis far field overpressure), AC 450.139-1 (toxic hazards for flight and ground), AC 450.143-1 (reduced reliability flight safety system), AC 450.169-1 with draft AC 450.169-1A (collision avoidance), AC 450.173-1 (mishap plan), and AC 450.179-1 (ground safety). Pre application consultation is codified in 14 CFR 413.5 and operationalized through the FAA Pre Application Consultation Checklist, not a numbered AC. Lightning hazard mitigation under 450.163 has no AC; the accepted means of compliance is NASA-STD 4010.
h1: FAA Part 450 advisory circulars: the complete AC 450 series guide
meta_title: FAA AC 450 Series: Complete Part 450 Advisory Circulars List
meta_description: Complete list of FAA Part 450 advisory circulars (AC 450 series) with section mapping, issue dates, and primary faa.gov sources for commercial launch licensing.

sections.heading: Pre application consultation and application completeness
paragraphs: Every Part 450 license application begins outside the AC 450 series proper. The pre application consultation step is mandated by 14 CFR 413.5, not by a Part 450 AC. Industry occasionally refers to a non existent AC 450.5-1, but the FAA has never published a document under that number. The operative tool is the FAA Pre Application Consultation Checklist hosted on the AST Streamlined Licensing Process page, which structures the pre application dialogue around entrance and exit criteria, scope of the license, means of compliance for each Part 450 requirement, safety element approvals, and the 180 day review sequence.

Once the consultation closes, the applicant must clear two formal gates established by AC 413.13-1, Guidance on Submitting a Complete Enough and Complete Application for a Vehicle Operator License (Dec 18, 2023). The first gate, complete enough, contains the minimum content needed for AST to begin technical review. Below this bar, FAA returns the package without starting the 180 day clock under 14 CFR 413.15. The second gate, complete, contains all material needed for the FAA to issue a license or denial within that statutory window.

AC 413.13-1 catalogs the regulatory anchors each application section must address: system safety under 450.103, means of compliance acceptance, hazard controls, flight safety analyses across 450.115 to 450.139, ground safety across 450.171 to 450.189, human spaceflight under Part 460, and the policy and payload screens of 450.31, 450.41, and 450.43. It is paired with the Part 413 License Application Procedures Checklist, also on faa.gov.
bullets: 14 CFR 413.5: pre application consultation requirement
FAA Pre Application Consultation Checklist: operational guide
AC 413.13-1 (Dec 18, 2023): complete enough and complete gates
AC 450.3-1: scope of a vehicle operator license, definition of a launch under 450.3
AC 450.31-1 (Aug 10, 2023): Applying for FAA Determination on Policy or Payload Reviews under 450.31, 450.41, and 450.43

sections.heading: System safety and flight safety analysis methods
paragraphs: Four ACs anchor the safety case at the heart of any Part 450 application. AC 450.103-1, System Safety Program (Sep 7, 2021), provides one acceptable method for satisfying 14 CFR 450.103. It is organized around four pillars drawn from the rule: a safety organization with a designated safety official holding stop work authority, a hazard management process that tracks hazards, risks, mitigations, and verifications, configuration management of all safety critical hardware, software, procedures, and analyses, and post flight data review with internal accident and anomaly investigation.

AC 450.108-1, Flight Abort Rule Development (Jul 27, 2021), implements 14 CFR 450.108. It introduces the quantitative gate structure that ties flight safety system (FSS) reliability to public risk consequence. Maximum conditional expected casualties (CEC) for reasonably foreseeable failure modes drives the choice: above 1x10^-2, the operator must field a Highly Reliable Flight Safety System per 14 CFR 450.145; between 1x10^-3 and 1x10^-2, a Reduced Reliability Flight Safety System per 14 CFR 450.143 is required; below 1x10^-3, abort by FSS is not compelled by 450.108(b).

AC 450.115-1B, High Fidelity Flight Safety Analysis (Aug 2, 2024), supersedes AC 450.115-1A and is the accepted means of compliance for 14 CFR 450.115(b) when a high fidelity FSA is required. Its core architecture is a Monte Carlo malfunction trajectory simulation: failure modes are enumerated, propagated as malfunction trajectories, debris is dispersed, and impact distributions are convolved with population data to yield expected casualty and individual risk metrics. AC 450.115-2 (Sep 20, 2024) addresses the documentation side of 450.115(c): the FSA method description itself, with scientific principles, statistical methods, assumptions, and validation evidence under 450.101(g).

subsections.heading: Issued ACs in this group
bullets: AC 450.103-1, System Safety Program (Sep 7, 2021)
AC 450.108-1, Flight Abort Rule Development (Jul 27, 2021)
AC 450.109-1, Flight Hazard Analysis
AC 450.115-1B, High Fidelity Flight Safety Analysis (Aug 2, 2024)
AC 450.115-2, Describing Flight Safety Analysis Methods (Sep 20, 2024)
AC 450.117-1, Trajectory Analysis for Normal Flight (Aug 19, 2021)
AC 450.143-1, Reduced Reliability Flight Safety System (Federal Register notice May 14, 2025)

sections.heading: Population exposure, far field overpressure, and toxic hazards
paragraphs: AC 450.123-1, Population Exposure Assessment (Oct 12, 2022), implements 14 CFR 450.123. It requires the operator to capture spatial and temporal population distribution at a resolution sufficient for risk integration, to distribute people across structure and vehicle classes for vulnerability inputs to casualty modeling under 450.135, to use reliable source data (LandScan or equivalents), and to address vulnerability to debris, blast, and thermal effects. The applicant submits both a method description tied to 450.115(c) and tabular exposure data covering uncontrolled, controlled, and waterborne populations.

AC 450.137-1, De Minimis Far Field Overpressure Blast Effects Analysis (Sep 10, 2024), supports 14 CFR 450.137. It offers a screening method: if energetic yield, distance to the nearest exposed population, and propagation conditions place the far field blast overpressure casualty contribution below FAA's negligibility threshold, the applicant may truncate the full quantitative analysis. The screening uses the ANSI S2.20-1983 glass damage model for window breakage probability, the dominant injury pathway in far field events.

AC 450.139-1, Toxic Hazards (Jul 8, 2024), is the single AC covering both 14 CFR 450.139 (flight) and 14 CFR 450.187 (ground). It tells operators how to identify whether toxic materials are present in the propulsion system, ordnance, or combustion products, how to demonstrate non applicability when none are, and how to arrange for a Federal entity such as a DoD range to perform the toxic release hazard analysis (TRHA). The current version carves out kerosene based fuels (RP-1, Jet A) from TRHA unless the Administrator determines otherwise. Higher fidelity dispersion modeling is reserved for a future revision.
bullets: AC 450.123-1, Population Exposure Assessment (Oct 12, 2022): implements 450.123
AC 450.137-1, De Minimis Far Field Overpressure (Sep 10, 2024): screening per ANSI S2.20-1983
AC 450.139-1, Toxic Hazards (Jul 8, 2024): covers both 450.139 flight and 450.187 ground

sections.heading: Lightning, flight commit criteria, and collision avoidance
paragraphs: Lightning hazard mitigation under 14 CFR 450.163 has no Advisory Circular. The FAA Part 450 Subpart C Accepted Means of Compliance Table v3 (Sep 11, 2024) lists the accepted means of compliance for 450.163(a)(1) as NASA-STD 4010, Lightning Launch Commit Criteria for Space Flight, plus the legacy Appendix G to Part 417. The AC column for 450.163 is N/A. Operators meet 450.163 by adopting NASA-STD 4010, not a Part 450 AC.

Flight commit criteria under 14 CFR 450.165 also have no standalone AC. The Means of Compliance Table shows no AC and no other accepted means of compliance for 450.165, so flight commit criteria are addressed operator by operator in the license application. Operators sometimes confuse 450.165 with 450.108 Flight Abort; the published flight abort guidance is AC 450.108-1, not a hypothetical AC 450.165-1.

Collision avoidance is covered by AC 450.169-1, Launch and Reentry Collision Avoidance Analysis (Aug 10, 2023), signed by Associate Administrator Kelvin B. Coleman. It explains the collision analysis worksheet under Appendix A to Part 450, identifies the 19th Space Defense Squadron as the COLA processing entity, and describes how the operator submits the worksheet and responds to the analysis returned. On Sep 17, 2025 the FAA published Federal Register notice 90 FR 44871, FR Doc. 2025-17922, Docket FAA-2025-2928, proposing AC 450.169-1A. The single substantive change is to add the Trajectory Operations and Planning Officer (TOPO) at Johnson Space Center for the International Space Station and supporting vehicles, and the Conjunction Assessment Risk Analysis (CARA) program at Goddard Space Flight Center for NASA robotic missions not involving human spaceflight, as approved COLA providers alongside the 19th SDS. Comments closed Oct 17, 2025; AC 450.169-1A remained a draft as of late 2025.
bullets: 450.163 Lightning hazard mitigation: no AC; means of compliance is NASA-STD 4010
450.165 Flight commit criteria: no AC; operator specific in application
AC 450.108-1 (May 25, 2021): Flight Abort Rule Development, not 450.165
AC 450.169-1 (Aug 10, 2023): COLA, 19th SDS as processing entity
AC 450.169-1A draft (Sep 17, 2025, 90 FR 44871): adds TOPO at JSC and CARA at GSFC

sections.heading: Ground safety, disposal, and the mishap plan
paragraphs: The FAA consolidated the ground safety topics into AC 450.179-1, Ground Safety. It maps to 14 CFR 450.179 (general ground safety), 450.181 (coordination with a site operator), 450.183 (explosive site plan), 450.185 (ground hazard analysis), and 450.189 (ground safety prescribed hazard controls). The AC incorporates DoD Explosives Safety Board methods under DoD 6055.09-M and Air Force Manual 91-201 conventions for net explosive weight, hazard division, and inhabited building distance, and applies MIL-STD-882E system safety practice to hazard identification, cause analysis, severity and likelihood ranking, and verification of controls.

Disposal hazard analysis is not a separate AC. It is folded into AC 450.179-1's treatment of 450.189 prescribed controls, which cover countdown abort, emergency shutdown, hazardous operations procedures, post flight safing, de servicing, and disposal of energetic, pressurized, or toxic residuals. Ground toxic hazards under 450.187 are handled by AC 450.139-1, which covers both flight and ground toxics in a single document and gives operators two compliance paths: toxic containment under 450.187(d) or toxic risk assessment under 450.187(e), against the 450.101 collective and individual public risk criteria.

AC 450.173-1, Part 450 Mishap Plan, Reporting, Response, and Investigation Requirements, implements 14 CFR 450.173 and requires a written FAA approved mishap plan before any licensed launch or reentry. It addresses plan contents under 450.173(b) through (g), reporting timelines (immediate notification to the FAA Washington Operations Center for any mishap meeting the 401.7 definition, a preliminary written report within five days, and a final report with corrective actions before return to flight), response procedures for debris, toxic plumes, fire, and evacuation, and investigation methodology coordinated with NTSB practice.
bullets: No separate AC 450.181-1 (coordination) or AC 450.187-1 (ground toxics)
AC 450.139-1 covers ground toxic hazards under 450.187 alongside flight toxics under 450.139
AC 450.173-1, Mishap Plan: implements 450.173, references 401.7 mishap definition
No AC 450.219-1; 450.219 of 14 CFR concerns records, not mishaps

sections.heading: AC issuance hierarchy: one AC per Part 450 rule, with consolidations
paragraphs: The AC 450 series is not a one to one mapping of every Part 450 section. The FAA's working pattern is to publish one AC per regulatory topic, sometimes consolidating multiple adjacent sections, and to leave a section without an AC when an existing industry standard already serves as the accepted means of compliance. Three patterns explain most apparent gaps:

First, single AC covers multiple sections. AC 450.179-1 covers five ground safety sections (450.179, 450.181, 450.183, 450.185, 450.189). AC 450.139-1 covers two rules across flight and ground (450.139 and 450.187). AC 450.31-1 covers three policy and payload sections (450.31, 450.41, 450.43). AC 450.115-1B and AC 450.115-2 together cover 450.115 from two angles: the analysis itself and the description of methods.

Second, external standard substitutes for AC. The most cited example is 14 CFR 450.163 lightning, where NASA-STD 4010 is the listed accepted means of compliance and no AC exists. The Means of Compliance Table is the authoritative tracker of these substitutions.

Third, no published AC and no accepted external means; the rule is met case by case. 14 CFR 450.165 flight commit criteria sits here. The applicant proposes a method during pre application consultation, and the FAA accepts or rejects it. Equivalent Level of Safety (ELOS) follows a similar logic, with the most developed reference inside AC 450.101-1B (May 3, 2024) covering High Consequence Event Protection under 450.101.

sections.heading: Where to find the accepted means of compliance table and AC index
paragraphs: The two FAA pages an applicant should bookmark before drafting compliance documentation are the Commercial Space Advisory Circulars index and the Part 450 Subpart C Accepted Means of Compliance Table. The index lists every AC issued by AST and is the canonical source for the AC 450 series. The Means of Compliance Table v3 (Sep 11, 2024) is the authoritative single page mapping each Part 450 section to its accepted AC, accepted external standard (such as NASA-STD 4010 for 450.163), or the absence of either.

Draft ACs in public comment, like AC 450.169-1A as of late 2025, appear in the FAA's draft ACs area and are formally noticed through the Federal Register. Workshops on individual ACs (Ground Safety, Mishap Plan, Toxic Hazards, and others) are linked from the AST Streamlined Licensing Process page and provide briefing decks that supplement the AC text.

Outside the AC series, three further FAA artifacts are commonly required: the Part 413 License Application Procedures Checklist that pairs with AC 413.13-1, the Pre Application Consultation Checklist required by 14 CFR 413.5, and the AST-1 office filter in the Advisory Circular search facet for cross referencing AST signatures and dates. All are hosted on faa.gov.
bullets: FAA Commercial Space ACs index: master list of issued ACs
Part 450 Means of Compliance Table v3 (Sep 11, 2024): section to AC mapping
FAA Draft ACs area: documents in public comment
AST Streamlined Licensing Process page: workshops, checklists, applicant resources
Federal Register: official AC notices and docket numbers

faqs.question: What is the FAA AC 450 series?
faqs.answer: The FAA AC 450 series is the set of Advisory Circulars published by the FAA Office of Commercial Space Transportation (AST) that provide accepted means of compliance with 14 CFR Part 450, the performance based rule governing commercial launch and reentry vehicle operator licenses in the United States. Each AC maps to one or more sections of Part 450 and is signed under AST-1 authority. The ACs are non binding; an applicant may propose alternative means of compliance for FAA acceptance. The complete current list and any drafts in public comment are tracked on the FAA's Commercial Space Advisory Circulars index page on faa.gov.

faqs.question: Is there an AC for every section of 14 CFR Part 450?
faqs.answer: No. The FAA publishes one AC per topic rather than one AC per regulatory section, and some sections have no AC at all. AC 450.179-1 alone covers five ground safety sections (450.179, 450.181, 450.183, 450.185, 450.189). AC 450.139-1 covers both flight toxics (450.139) and ground toxics (450.187). For 14 CFR 450.163 lightning mitigation, the accepted means of compliance is NASA-STD 4010, not an AC. For 14 CFR 450.165 flight commit criteria, there is no AC and no external standard; operators address it case by case during pre application consultation.

faqs.question: Does AC 450.5-1 exist?
faqs.answer: No. There is no Advisory Circular numbered AC 450.5-1 in the FAA Commercial Space AC library. Pre application consultation is not a Part 450 obligation; it is codified in 14 CFR 413.5, which makes consultation with the FAA mandatory before submitting a license application. The operational guidance is the FAA Part 450 Pre Application Consultation Checklist published on the AST Streamlined Licensing Process page. The checklist covers entrance and exit criteria, intended means of compliance for each Part 450 requirement, scope of the license, safety element approvals, and how the 180 day review will be sequenced.

faqs.question: What does AC 413.13-1 require for a complete application?
faqs.answer: AC 413.13-1, dated Dec 18, 2023, defines two gates for a Part 450 vehicle operator license application. The complete enough gate requires the minimum content needed for AST to begin technical review; without it, the package is returned and the 180 day clock under 14 CFR 413.15 does not start. The complete gate requires all material needed for the FAA to issue a license or denial within the 180 day window. The AC catalogs anchors across 450.103 (system safety), 450.115 to 450.139 (FSA), 450.171 to 450.189 (ground safety), Part 460 (human spaceflight), and 450.31, 450.41, 450.43 (policy and payload).

faqs.question: What CEC thresholds does AC 450.108-1 use for flight safety system reliability?
faqs.answer: AC 450.108-1, Flight Abort Rule Development (Jul 27, 2021), ties flight safety system (FSS) reliability to maximum conditional expected casualties (CEC) for reasonably foreseeable failure modes. If CEC exceeds 1x10^-2 in any significant period of flight in uncontrolled areas, the operator must field a Highly Reliable Flight Safety System (HRFSS) meeting 14 CFR 450.145. If CEC is between 1x10^-3 and 1x10^-2, a Reduced Reliability Flight Safety System (RRFSS) meeting 14 CFR 450.143 is required. Below 1x10^-3, flight abort via an FSS is not compelled by 450.108(b). AC 450.108-1 pairs with AC 450.115-1B for the underlying analysis.

faqs.question: Where is the Equivalent Level of Safety (ELOS) guidance for Part 450?
faqs.answer: ELOS guidance is not in AC 450.31-1, despite occasional misattribution. AC 450.31-1 is titled Applying for FAA Determination on Policy or Payload Reviews and implements 14 CFR 450.31, 450.41, and 450.43. ELOS for Part 450 lives in the section specific ACs that implement quantitative safety requirements, most prominently AC 450.101-1B, Public Safety: High Consequence Event Protection (May 3, 2024), which discusses how an operator may propose an alternative approach delivering an equivalent level of safety to the conditional expected casualty thresholds in 14 CFR 450.101. The earlier AC 450.101-1 and AC 450.101-1A have been superseded.

faqs.question: What is the difference between AC 450.115-1B and AC 450.115-2?
faqs.answer: AC 450.115-1B, High Fidelity Flight Safety Analysis (Aug 2, 2024), is the engineering recipe: how to run Monte Carlo malfunction trajectory simulations, propagate debris, convolve impact distributions with population data, and produce expected casualty and individual risk metrics in compliance with 14 CFR 450.115(b). It superseded AC 450.115-1A. AC 450.115-2, Describing Flight Safety Analysis Methods (Sep 20, 2024), addresses the documentation side of 450.115(c): how the operator describes the methods, scientific principles, assumptions, fidelity rationale, and validation and verification evidence under 450.101(g). Together they cover both the calculation and the auditable writeup of the FSA.

faqs.question: What does AC 450.169-1A change about collision avoidance?
faqs.answer: AC 450.169-1A is a draft revision of AC 450.169-1, Launch and Reentry Collision Avoidance Analysis (Aug 10, 2023). The FAA published the Notice of Availability and Request for Comments in 90 FR 44871 on Sep 17, 2025 (FR Doc. 2025-17922, Docket FAA-2025-2928). The single substantive change is to add two NASA components as approved COLA providers alongside the 19th Space Defense Squadron: the Trajectory Operations and Planning Officer (TOPO) at Johnson Space Center for the International Space Station and supporting vehicles, and the Conjunction Assessment Risk Analysis (CARA) program at Goddard Space Flight Center for NASA robotic missions. Comments closed Oct 17, 2025.

faqs.question: Is there an AC for ground toxic hazards under 14 CFR 450.187?
faqs.answer: There is no separate AC 450.187-1. The FAA addresses both flight toxics (14 CFR 450.139) and ground toxics (14 CFR 450.187) in a single document, AC 450.139-1, Toxic Hazards, dated Jul 8, 2024. The AC covers identifying whether toxic materials are present in propulsion, ordnance, or combustion products, demonstrating non applicability when none are, and arranging for a Federal entity such as a DoD range to perform a toxic release hazard analysis (TRHA). RP-1 and Jet A are carved out from TRHA unless the Administrator determines otherwise. Higher fidelity dispersion modeling is reserved for a future revision.

faqs.question: What does the FAA mishap plan AC require?
faqs.answer: AC 450.173-1, Part 450 Mishap Plan, Reporting, Response, and Investigation Requirements, implements 14 CFR 450.173 and requires a written FAA approved mishap plan before any licensed launch or reentry. The plan must define roles, allocation between launch operator and site operator, emergency response activation, evidence preservation, investigation processes, root cause determination, corrective actions, and return to flight gating. Reporting timelines include immediate notification to the FAA Washington Operations Center for any mishap meeting the 14 CFR 401.7 definition, a preliminary written report within five days, and a final report with corrective actions before the next operation unless the Administrator approves otherwise.

faqs.question: Where do I find the FAA accepted means of compliance table for Part 450?
faqs.answer: The Part 450 Subpart C Accepted Means of Compliance Table is hosted on faa.gov. The current version is v3, dated Sep 11, 2024. It is the authoritative single page mapping each Part 450 section to its accepted AC, its accepted external standard (for example NASA-STD 4010 for 14 CFR 450.163 lightning), or the explicit absence of either. The Commercial Space ACs index complements the table by listing every issued AC, with PDFs and document IDs. Draft ACs in public comment, such as AC 450.169-1A as of late 2025, appear in the FAA draft ACs area and are noticed in the Federal Register.

faqs.question: What population exposure data sources does AC 450.123-1 accept?
faqs.answer: AC 450.123-1, Population Exposure Assessment (Oct 12, 2022), implements 14 CFR 450.123 and requires the operator to use reliable, accurate, and timely population data at spatial and temporal resolution sufficient to support risk integration. The AC discusses LandScan and equivalent gridded population databases, distribution of people across structure classes (residential, commercial) and vehicle types (motor vehicle, marine, aircraft) for vulnerability inputs to casualty modeling under 14 CFR 450.135, and explicit treatment of population vulnerability to debris, blast, and thermal effects. The application package under 450.123(c) must include both the method description and tabular exposure data for uncontrolled, controlled, and waterborne populations.
